does: This paper introduces password-locked models as a novel evaluation framework for assessing capability elicitation methods. The authors create large language models that are deliberately trained to hide certain capabilities behind a password mechanism—they only exhibit these abilities when a specific password appears in the prompt, otherwise imitating a weaker model. They then evaluate whether standard elicitation techniques, particularly fine-tuning and in-context learning with demonstrations, can unlock these hidden capabilities without access to the password. Key findings show that a small number of high-quality demonstrations are often sufficient to fully elicit password-locked capabilities, and that fine-tuning can recover capabilities even when the original password-locking mechanism should theoretically prevent this.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on AI safety evaluation and capability assessment of large language models. It extends prior research on adversarial prompting, jailbreaking techniques, and methods for uncovering hidden model abilities that simple prompting fails to reveal. The paper responds to the challenge that capability elicitation methods, including fine-tuning-based approaches, remain poorly understood in their effectiveness and scope. It connects to broader work on model transparency, behavioral testing frameworks, and the fundamental problem of determining whether safety training and behavioral constraints are robust to various inference-time attacks or training-time modification.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more rigorous evaluation of capability elicitation defenses and safety measures in large language models. By establishing password-locked models as a controlled testing ground, future work can develop and benchmark elicitation-resistant alignment techniques, test whether multiple independent safety training approaches actually prevent capability recovery, and investigate the theoretical limits of hiding capabilities in neural networks. The approach enables researchers to quantify how much behavioral control can be maintained over fine-tuned models and to develop stronger methods for ensuring that safety properties remain robust under realistic downstream use cases. This contributes to the broader project of building provably safer AI systems.
